FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data, N=10,054, ages 12-13 years, Year 3 (2019-2021)

FT_IC2.evidence: Outcomes are R-rated movie consumption and mature-rated video game use; exposures are parenting practices (screen time modeling, mealtime screen use, bedroom screen use, monitoring, limiting), not smartphone/social media variables per se

FT_IC3.evidence: Does not meet FT_IC2, therefore IC3 is not applicable

FT_IC4.evidence: Does not meet FT_IC2, therefore IC4 is not applicable

FT_IC5.evidence: Does not meet FT_IC2, therefore IC5 is not applicable

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in BMC Pediatrics, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Paper does not meet FT_IC2 (no smartphone/SM exposure variable), so FT_EC7 directional check not applicable
notes: Paper focuses on parenting practices as predictors of mature media consumption. While it uses ABCD data and is a journal article, the exposure is parenting behavior not smartphone/social media use, which fails to meet FT_IC2 inclusion criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data, N=9,519, longitudinal from baseline 2016-2018 (ages 9-10) to Year 3 2019-2021 (ages 12-13)

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple specific screen time variables measured: TV shows/movies, video games (single and multiplayer), digital socializing (texting, video chatting, social networking). Self-reported daily hours per weekday and weekend, calculated as weighted average

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal mixed-effects modeling with compositional data analysis (CoDA) examining associations between screen time and sleep, sports/exercise activity over four years with isometric log-ratio transformations

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric values reported: screen time increased by 0.23 h/day (95% CI 0.22-0.24) baseline to Year 1, 1.58 h/day (95% CI 1.53-1.63) to Year 2, 3.28 h/day (95% CI 3.10-3.46) to Year 3. TV shows/movies 0.96 h/day females, 0.92 males; video games 1.00 h/day females, 1.65 males; digital socializing 1.39 h/day females, 0.48 males

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis examining four annual assessments with compositional data analysis and mixed-effects modeling accounting for age, COVID-19 pandemic period, and baseline demographics

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in BMC Pediatrics, 2026

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes multiple outcomes (screen time, sleep, sports/exercise activity) with phone/SM as one of several predictors, not solely as dependent variable. Also includes non-demographic predictors (age as continuous, COVID-19 period) and cross-sectional relationships within longitudinal framework. Phone/SM → sleep/activity outcomes tested.
notes: Strong longitudinal study with detailed screen time breakdown by modality. Uses compositional data analysis approach to model 24-hour movement behaviors. Clearly reports numeric statistics with confidence intervals across multiple timepoints and sex-stratified analyses.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (5.1 release), N=2,749 at Year 2, ages 11.9 years; analyzed Years 2, 3, and 4 follow-up data

FT_IC2.evidence: Gaming duration measured at Year 2 (daily hours of gaming) and problematic gaming measured at Year 2 (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire - VGAQ). Both are specific smartphone-related gaming variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Causal mediation analysis examining gaming duration/problems (Year 2) → caffeine use (Year 3) → sleep duration/difficulties (Year 4) with inferential tests on indirect, direct, and total effects

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric statistics reported: caffeine use partially mediated gaming-sleep difficulties relationship (indirect effect = 0.023, 95% CI [0.004, 0.049]); gaming-sleep duration (indirect effect = -0.006, 95% CI [-0.014, -0.001]); gaming-sleep difficulties direct effect = 0.036; gaming-sleep duration direct effect = -0.008

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal mediation analysis with three follow-up waves (Years 2, 3, 4), using causal mediation analysis in R package with maximum likelihood estimation and 5,000 bootstrap simulations

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Gaming is independent variable/predictor; sleep is dependent variable/outcome (not gaming as DV). Includes mediation analysis with intermediate outcome (caffeine). Sleep is a psychological/behavioral outcome beyond demographic predictors only.
notes: Well-designed longitudinal mediation study with prospective design across three years. Reports both indirect and direct effects with confidence intervals. Caffeine use serves as proposed mechanism linking gaming to sleep outcomes.
